title: O(n log n) Mixing Time Conjecture for Glauber Dynamics
statement_text: The Glauber dynamics for $k$-colourings of a graph $G$ with maximum degree $\Delta$ has mixing time $O(n \log n)$, provided that $k \geq \Delta + 2$.
context_text: Described as 'a famous conjecture in the area' in the introduction. Jerrum and independently Salas and Sokal proved rapid mixing for $k \geq 2\Delta$; Vigoda improved this to $k \geq 11\Delta/6$. The paper asks whether this $O(n \log n)$ bound can be extended to Glauber dynamics for $(\Delta+1)$-colourings restricted to the non-frozen component, and Proposition 3 answers in the negative for constant $\Delta$.
paper_contribution: Proves (Proposition 3) that the mixing time of Glauber dynamics for $(\Delta+1)$-colourings restricted to the non-frozen component can be at least $n^2/(8(\Delta+1))$, showing the $O(n\log n)$ bound cannot be extended to $k = \Delta+1$.
notes: No formal theorem environment; stated as 'a famous conjecture in the area' in prose without an explicit attributed citation label. PDF source.

title: Informal: Δ = o(n) condition not necessary for Corollary 2
statement_text: The condition $\Delta = o(n)$ in Corollary 2 is probably not necessary.
context_text: Corollary 2 states that for a graph on $n$ vertices with $\Delta = o(n)$, Glauber dynamics for $(\Delta+1)$-colourings starting at a uniformly random colouring gives an almost uniform sampler. The authors remark immediately after the corollary that the constraint $\Delta = o(n)$ is likely an artifact of the proof technique.
notes: Stated in running prose immediately after Corollary 2: 'The condition Δ = o(n) is probably not necessary.' PDF source.

title: Informal: Exponent in Theorem 1 not optimal
statement_text: The dependence on $\Delta$ in the exponent of the bound $\mathbb{P}(\alpha \text{ is frozen}) \leq (6/7)^{n/(\Delta+1)}$ in Theorem 1 is not best possible.
context_text: Theorem 1 gives the upper bound $(6/7)^{n/(\Delta+1)}$ on the probability a uniform random $(\Delta+1)$-colouring is frozen. Proposition 4 provides a matching lower bound with exponent $e^{-3\log(\Delta)/\Delta \cdot n}$, leaving a gap of a $\log\Delta$ factor. The authors believe the upper bound can be tightened to close this gap.
notes: Stated in running prose: 'We believe that the dependence on Δ in the exponent in Theorem 1 is not best possible.' PDF source — verify exponent expressions.
